topic: Report of the Ombudsman, OPCAT Report: Report on an examination of the Prisoners of Extreme Risk Unit under the Crimes of Torture Act 1989
description: Thursday 16 October 2025 | Justice Committee Report of the Ombudsman, OPCAT Report: Report on an examination of the Prisoners of Extreme Risk Unit under the Crimes of Torture Act 1989 Department of Corrections - Leigh Marsh, Commissioner Custodial Services - Jeanette Burns, Director Extreme Risk Directorate - Juanita Ryan, DCE Pae Ora - Emma Gardner, Chief Mental Health & Addictions Officer
descriptionSummary: Report of the Ombudsman, OPCAT Report: Report on an examination of the Prisoners of Extreme Risk Unit under the Crimes of Torture Act 1989. Department of Corrections. Leigh Marsh, Commissioner Custodial Services

Uh, I I'm gonna limit my opening remarks to just a few key themes and issues that are top of mind on the back of the Ombudsman's op cap report on the Prisoners Extreme Risk Unit. Uh, in the report, as you're aware, there were three recommendations, uh, to cease the use of the proof operating model, which we declined increased time outta cell and increased access to meaningful human contact, which we accepted and ensuring safe and humane, transparent

and fair management of people in custody, which we accepted subject to further work to implement our plan to increase time outta cell, increase meaningful human contact for the men in the Peru. And to address wider comments in the report, we established the Prisoners Extreme Risk Program or our per program. The program aims to ensure strategic and operational alignment of all work contributing to the future management of the people of extreme risk.

The response to the op cap report is a dedicated work stream within this program. The program also includes a dedicated through operating model work stream, which will review the current unit operating model and explore opportunities for continuous improvement. The prisons of at risk directorate and the prisons of extreme risk units were set up in 2019 to manage people who present an extreme risk to the safety of the public, our staff, and other people in prison.

The Pru has played a key role in promoting the safety and mitigating the significant risk posed by these individuals. The Peru is currently the most suitable unit to manage this risk. No serious staff or prisoner injuries have occurred in the Peru. Since its establishment support, these men are provided and the dedication shown by staff keeps everybody safe.

One of the complexities of the Peru is that it holds men with a broad range of risk areas, and we acknowledge there are some men in the Peru who because of the type of risk they pose, beyond the immediacy of violent extremism or interpersonal violence may be better managed in a different type of unit. These men have significant influence capability in terms of transnational organized crime or 10 o They are therefore not currently suitable to be managed in a mainstream or directed segregation unit.

As the committee is aware, the scope, scale and impact of transnational organized crime is growing in New Zealand. And this has had a significant impact on Ural environment. We've seen changes in prison dynamic dynamics resulting from this and the harm that can cause both inside and outside our prisons to the wide and to the wider New Zealand communities to help respond to the increasing enduring 10 o risks within the Ural environment.

And to help contribute to the whole of government response to tackling organized crime corrections has committed to establish a dedicated unit to accommodate and manage the highest risk 10 o individuals in our management. This specialized unit will be based at Auckland Prison, and it will have a physical environment that will range, uh, that will reflect a range of security classifications with dedicated and consistently highly trained staff to minimize opportunity for further offending and harm.

The Peru model in operation today is not the same as the one that was in place when the unit was established or at the time of the Ombudsman's inspection in 2023. The operating model of the Peru is regularly evolving in response to risk posed by those individuals who present an extreme threat or keep people in prison staff and the wider community.

Safe safety is our number one priority. The prisons can be volatile environments. We are constantly working to ensure the entire prison network provides the safest environment possible for our staff prisoners and our wider communities. With me today, I have Juanita Ryan, our Deputy Chief Executive of Piora Health and Rehabilitation. I have Jeanette Burns, director of Extreme Risk Directorate, and Emma Garner, our Chief Mental Health and Addictions Officer.
